The calves are considered the hardest muscle to grow, but scientists have recently discovered three solutions to their stubbornness. Jeff Nippard notes that the standing calf raise is significantly more effective than the seated calf raise. The gastrocnemius muscle grows more than 10 times more with standing versus seated calf raises, while the soleus muscle experiences the same amount of growth. This is because bending the knee reduces the stretch on the gastrocnemius. However, the key factor is not standing, but rather having a straight knee. 

A person should prioritize deep stretches when training the calves, as they require an even deeper stretch than previously thought. When performing calf raises, the exercise should be taken to the point where it is no longer possible to move the weight. Research has found that the calves experience better growth from doing tiny partial repetitions beyond failure, including 8th and 10th reps. The direction of the toes also plays a role, as pointing them straight ahead targets the entire calf evenly. Pointing the toes outward targets the inner calves more, while pointing them inward targets the outer calves more. Alternating between pointing the toes inward and outward may cause the best growth of both calf heads, according to researchers.
